passages.title: The Planets
passages.text:
Our solar system contains eight planets, each with unique characteristics. The four inner planets — Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars — are called terrestrial planets because they have solid, rocky surfaces. The four outer planets — Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune — are called gas giants because they are made mostly of gases.

Earth and Jupiter could hardly be more different. Earth has a solid surface where people can walk, liquid water in its oceans, and an atmosphere that supports life. Jupiter, on the other hand, has no solid surface at all. It is a swirling mass of gases with storms larger than our entire planet.

Despite these differences, both planets share some similarities. Both have magnetic fields that protect them from harmful solar radiation. Both have moons. And both orbit the same star.

questions.question: What is the main idea of paragraph 2?
choices: Earth has a solid surface and water
Jupiter has more gravity than Earth
Jupiter is more interesting to study
Earth and Jupiter have very different physical properties
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 lists differences between Earth and Jupiter.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the WHOLE passage?
choices: Planets are different but share some traits
Earth is the only special planet
questions.explanation: The passage covers BOTH differences and similarities.

wrongExplanations.2: Wrong — only outer planets are gas giants.

passages.title: Pointillism
passages.text:
In 1886, Georges Seurat shocked the art world with Pointillism — placing tiny dots of pure color side by side on canvas. From a distance, the eye blends these dots into colors that shimmer.

Not everyone appreciated this. Critics called it mechanical and tedious, claiming it removed emotion from painting.

Despite criticism, Seurat's work influenced generations of artists. His ideas about color theory became foundational in art education. Today, his paintings hang in major museums.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Pointillism was controversial but became influential
questions.explanation: The passage covers BOTH the criticism AND the influence.

wrongExplanations.3: OFF-TOPIC — not telling painters what to do.

passages.title: Octopus Brains
passages.text:
Octopuses have nine brains. One central brain coordinates the body, but each of its eight arms also has its own small brain.

This means each arm can solve problems on its own. Scientists have watched octopus arms open jars, untie knots, and even taste food independently.

This unusual nervous system gives octopuses special abilities — changing color in less than a second, slipping through tiny cracks, and using tools.

Studying octopus brains may help us understand intelligence in completely different ways than studying mammals.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the WHOLE passage?
choices: Octopuses have unusual brains and abilities
Octopus arms can survive on their own
questions.explanation: Covers brains AND abilities AND scientific interest.

questions.question: What is the main idea of paragraph 2 specifically?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 is about arms acting on their own.

passages.title: Why Bees Matter
passages.text:
Honeybees are one of the most important insects on Earth. They pollinate about one-third of all crops humans eat. Without bees, foods like apples, almonds, blueberries, and broccoli would be much harder to grow.

When a bee lands on a flower to drink nectar, pollen sticks to its body. As it flies to the next flower, it carries that pollen with it. This is what allows plants to make seeds.

Unfortunately, bee populations are dropping. Diseases, pesticides, and loss of wildflowers are all making it harder for bees to survive. Scientists are working to find solutions before it's too late.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the whole passage?
choices: Bees are important for our food but their numbers are dropping
questions.explanation: Covers importance AND decline together.

questions.question: What is the main idea of paragraph 2?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains the pollination process.

questions.question: What is paragraph 3 mostly about?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 lists threats to bees.

passages.title: The Library of Alexandria
passages.text:
Long ago, in ancient Egypt, the city of Alexandria was home to the most famous library in the world. Built more than 2,000 years ago, the Library of Alexandria held hundreds of thousands of scrolls — the books of that time.

Scholars from many countries traveled to Alexandria to study. They could read works on math, science, history, and poetry. Some say the library held copies of every book that existed.

Sadly, fires and wars destroyed the library over the centuries. Most of its scrolls were lost forever. Historians still wonder what knowledge disappeared with it.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: The Library of Alexandria was a great center of learning that was lost
questions.explanation: Covers the library's greatness AND its destruction.

questions.question: What is paragraph 2 mostly about?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 describes who visited and what they could read — the library's contents/users.

passages.title: How a Lighthouse Works
passages.text:
Lighthouses have guided ships for thousands of years. Their job is simple: warn sailors that land or rocks are nearby, especially at night or in fog.

A lighthouse uses a powerful light at the top of a tall tower. The light flashes in a special pattern — for example, three quick flashes, then a pause. Each lighthouse has its own pattern, so sailors know which lighthouse they are seeing.

Long ago, lighthouses used fires or oil lamps. Today, they use electric bulbs and computers. Some lighthouses have no keepers anymore — they run on their own.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Lighthouses guide ships using flashing patterns of light
questions.explanation: Covers purpose AND how they work (patterns, light).

wrongExplanations.3: WRONG — passage says some don't anymore.

questions.question: What is paragraph 2 about?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains the flash pattern signaling system.

passages.title: Coral Reefs
passages.text:
Coral reefs are sometimes called 'rainforests of the sea' because they support an incredible variety of ocean life. Though reefs cover less than 1% of the ocean floor, they are home to about 25% of all marine species.

Reefs are built by tiny animals called coral polyps. Over thousands of years, polyps build hard skeletons that grow into massive structures. Fish, turtles, sharks, and many other creatures live in and around these structures.

Today, coral reefs face serious problems. Warmer ocean temperatures cause coral 'bleaching,' which can kill the coral. Pollution and overfishing add to the threats. Many scientists worry that reefs may not survive without major changes.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the whole passage?
choices: Coral reefs are important ocean homes that are now threatened
questions.explanation: Covers importance AND threats — both are central.

questions.question: What is paragraph 1 mostly about?
choices: How much marine life lives in reefs
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says 25% of marine species live there.

questions.question: What is paragraph 3 about?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 lists bleaching, pollution, overfishing.

passages.title: The Wright Brothers
passages.text:
Wilbur and Orville Wright owned a bicycle shop in Ohio in the late 1800s. But their real passion was something else: they dreamed of building a flying machine.

At the time, no one had ever built a powered airplane that could carry a person. Many tried and failed. The Wright brothers studied bird flight, designed wings, and built models. They tested everything carefully.

In December 1903, on a windy beach in North Carolina, their work paid off. Their airplane, the Wright Flyer, flew 120 feet in 12 seconds. It doesn't sound like much today, but it changed the world. From that small flight came the entire age of aviation.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: The Wrights owned a bike shop
The Wright brothers were the first to build a powered airplane
questions.explanation: The whole passage tells the story of their first powered flight.

wrongExplanations.3: TOO NARROW — true but doesn't capture the brothers.

questions.question: What is paragraph 2 about?
choices: Their preparation and study before flying
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 covers their study and testing.

passages.title: Earthquakes
passages.text:
Earthquakes happen when huge sections of the Earth's crust suddenly slip past each other. The crust is made of giant pieces called tectonic plates, which are always moving slowly. Sometimes they get stuck. When they finally slip, the energy released causes the ground to shake.

Most earthquakes are too small to feel. But powerful earthquakes can damage buildings, break roads, and even cause tsunamis. Scientists called seismologists study earthquakes to understand them better.

While we cannot prevent earthquakes, we can prepare. Buildings in earthquake zones are designed to sway without falling. Schools practice 'drop, cover, and hold on' drills. These efforts save lives.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Earthquakes — what they are, what they do, and how we prepare
questions.explanation: Covers cause, effect, and preparation — all three paragraphs.

questions.question: What does paragraph 1 mainly explain?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 explains tectonic plates causing earthquakes.

questions.question: What is paragraph 3 about?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 talks about how we prepare.

passages.title: Penguins
passages.text:
Penguins are unusual birds — they cannot fly. But they are excellent swimmers. Their wings have evolved into flippers that work like fins underwater. A penguin can swim faster than most fish.

Most penguins live in the Southern Hemisphere, especially Antarctica. They survive bitter cold thanks to two layers: thick feathers on the outside and a layer of fat called blubber underneath.

Penguins live in large groups called colonies. Some colonies have hundreds of thousands of birds. They huddle together to stay warm in winter, taking turns moving from the cold edges to the warm center.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
questions.explanation: Covers swimming, surviving cold, and colonies.

questions.question: Paragraph 2 is mainly about what?
choices: Where penguins live and how they stay warm
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 covers location AND warmth (feathers, blubber).

passages.title: The Invention of Paper
passages.text:
Before paper existed, people wrote on heavy materials: stone, clay tablets, animal skins, and tree bark. None were ideal. Stone was too heavy to carry; bark fell apart; animal skin was expensive.

In ancient China, around 2,000 years ago, an inventor named Cai Lun figured out how to make paper from wood pulp and old rags. The result was light, cheap, and could be made in large quantities. It changed how people stored and shared information.

The secret of paper-making spread slowly across the world. It reached Europe a thousand years after Cai Lun's invention. Today, we make billions of pieces of paper every year, but we owe it all to that ancient breakthrough.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Cai Lun's invention of paper changed how humans share information
questions.explanation: Covers Cai Lun's invention and its impact.

questions.question: Paragraph 1 is mostly about what?
choices: What people used before paper, and why each was bad
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 lists pre-paper materials and their problems.

passages.title: The Power of Wind
passages.text:
Wind has been put to work by humans for thousands of years. Ancient sailors used wind to push boats across oceans. Farmers used windmills to grind grain.

Today, wind is used to make electricity. Tall towers called wind turbines have blades that spin in the wind. The spinning powers a generator that creates electricity. The electricity is sent through wires to homes and businesses.

Wind is a clean energy source — it doesn't pollute the air or water. But it has limits. Wind doesn't blow all the time. So we still need other energy sources to fill the gaps. Still, wind power is growing fast around the world.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the whole passage?
choices: Humans have used wind for ages, and now use it to make electricity
questions.explanation: Covers history and modern uses.

questions.question: Paragraph 3 is about what?
choices: The benefits and limits of wind energy
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 covers clean energy AND wind not blowing constantly.

questions.question: What is paragraph 2 about?
choices: How modern wind turbines make electricity
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains the modern turbine + generator system.

passages.title: Volcanoes
passages.text:
Volcanoes are mountains with a special feature: they can erupt. Deep underground, melted rock called magma builds up. When pressure gets too high, it bursts through the surface.

Volcanic eruptions can be small or huge. Some volcanoes leak lava slowly. Others explode with great force, sending ash, rocks, and gas miles into the air. The biggest eruptions can change weather around the world for years.

Despite their danger, volcanoes also do good. The cooled lava breaks down into rich soil that plants love. Many volcanic islands are some of the most fertile places on Earth.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Volcanoes — how they work, their power, and their benefits
questions.explanation: Covers cause, range of eruptions, and benefits.

questions.question: Paragraph 3 is about what?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says soil from volcanoes is rich.

passages.title: Why We Sleep
passages.text:
Every animal sleeps. Even fish. Why? For a long time, scientists weren't sure. But research has revealed several reasons.

First, sleep helps the body repair itself. Cells fix damage, growth hormones do their work, and the immune system gets stronger. Without enough sleep, you're more likely to get sick.

Second, sleep helps the brain. While you sleep, your brain sorts through what you learned that day. Memories get stored. New connections form. Without sleep, you can't focus, can't learn well, and can't remember.

Finally, sleep gives the brain time to clean itself. During sleep, fluid washes through the brain, removing waste from the day. Without this cleanup, brain damage builds up over time.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Sleep is important for body and brain in many ways
questions.explanation: Covers all benefits of sleep.

questions.question: Paragraph 2 is mainly about what?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 covers cells, hormones, immune system.

questions.question: What does paragraph 4 explain?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 covers the brain washing itself.

passages.title: Black Holes
passages.text:
A black hole is a place in space where gravity is so strong that nothing — not even light — can escape. Once something falls in, it's gone forever.

Black holes form when massive stars run out of fuel and collapse on themselves. The star's matter gets crushed into a tiny point with infinite density. The space around it bends in strange ways.

For a long time, black holes were just a theory. Even Einstein wasn't sure they really existed. But in 2019, scientists captured the first photo of a black hole, proving they're real. The image showed a glowing ring of gas around a dark center — the 'shadow' of the black hole itself.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: What black holes are, how they form, and proof they exist
questions.explanation: Covers definition, formation, and the proof.

questions.question: Paragraph 3 is about what?
choices: The 2019 photograph of a black hole
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 covers the proof: 2019 photo.

passages.title: The Brain's Speed
passages.text:
Your brain is faster than you might think. When you see something — a ball flying toward you, for example — your brain processes the image in less than 13 thousandths of a second. That's faster than a camera flash.

This speed lets you react. By the time you 'realize' you've seen the ball, your hand may already be moving to catch it. The brain handles the work before you're even conscious of it.

Different parts of the brain handle different tasks. The visual cortex sees. The motor cortex moves muscles. The two work together with very little delay. That's why athletes who practice can make split-second moves seem effortless.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: The brain processes information incredibly fast
Visual cortex is in the back
questions.explanation: All paragraphs are about brain speed.

questions.question: Paragraph 2 mainly explains what?
choices: How brain speed lets you react
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 connects speed to reaction.

passages.title: Forests of the Sea
passages.text:
Underwater, there are real forests. They are made of giant kelp — a type of seaweed that can grow over 100 feet tall. Kelp forests are found in cool ocean waters along many coastlines.

Like land forests, kelp forests provide food and shelter. Sea otters, fish, crabs, and many other animals live among the swaying stalks. Some animals depend on kelp completely.

Kelp grows incredibly fast — sometimes 18 inches a day. It needs sunlight, so it floats up toward the surface. The forest you see is the top of plants anchored far below.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Kelp forests are large undersea ecosystems
questions.explanation: All three paragraphs are about kelp forests.

questions.question: Paragraph 2 is mainly about what?
choices: Animals that live in kelp forests
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 lists animals that live there.
